builders.name: Aaron Levie
builders.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
One corollary to the fact that AI agents take real work to setup in company at scale, is that the role of the forward deployed engineer -or whatever it gets called in the future- isn’t going away any time soon. 

When a vendor sells any kind of agents into an organization, you’re no longer just selling a software tool that gets implemented and you’re done. You’re fundamentally selling some form of the actual workflow being done by your technology. This is far closer to a customer buying from a professional services firm than implementing traditional technology.

This will almost always require a deep understanding of the domain that the customer operates in, the ability to help a customer wire up their systems to support the agents, make sure all the context is setup in the right way, and help provide change management to actually get the company to adapt its business processes.

The ability to do this across customers, figure out best practices in a specific industry and customer segment, take new features back to go build in the product, and so on is going to be key. There’s no shortcut to getting this work done by the enterprise, and the vendors are going to have to do a lot of this or risk low adoption.

Finally, this is a big opportunity for existing and next gen professional services companies. There are all new practice areas emerging in every system integrator and consulting firm just to do this kind of work, and this is going to continue to be in demand for quite some time. Yet another example of jobs that aren’t actually going away.

The more enterprises I talk to about AI agent transformation, the more it’s clear that there is going to be a new type of role in most enterprises going forward. The job is to be the agent deployer and manager in teams. Here’s the rough JD:

This person will need to figure out what are the highest leverage set of workflows on a team are (either existing or new ones) where agents can actually drive significantly more value for the team and company. 

In general, it’s going to be in areas where if you threw compute (in the form of agents) at a task you could either execute it 100X faster or do it 100X more times than before. Examples would be processing orders of magnitude more leads to hand them off to reps with extra customer signal, automating a contracting review and intake process, streamlining a client onboarding process to reduce as many straps as possible, setting up knowledge bases than the whole company taps into, and so on.

This person’s job is to figure out what the future state workflow needs to look like to drive this new form of automation, and how to connect up the various existing or new systems in such a way that this can be fulfilled. The gnarly part of the work is mapping structured and unstructured data flows, figuring out the ideal workflow, getting the agent the context it needs to do the work properly, figuring out where the human interfaces with the agent and at what steps, manages evals and reviews after any major model or data change, and runs and manages the agents on an ongoing basis tracking KPIs, and so on.

The person must be good at mapping the process and understanding where the value could be unlocked and be relatively technical, and has full autonomy to connect up business systems and drive automation. This means they’re comfortable with skills, MCP, CLIs, and so on, and the company believes it’s safe for them to do so. But also great operationally and at business.

It may be an existing person repositioned, or a totally net new person in the company. There will likely need to be one or more of these people on every team, so it’s not a centralized role per se. It may rile up into IT or an AI team, or live in the function and just have checkpoints with a central function.

This would also be a fantastic job for next gen hires who are leaning into AI, and are technical, to be able to go into. And for anyone concerned about engineers in the future, this will be an obvious area for these skills as well.

builders.name: Zara Zhang
builders.bio: Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: https://t.co/KCuEajezlL YouTube: https://t.co/8xzbGWtf6w

tweets.text:
I think “play” should be taken more seriously

If you work in AI, playing with the models is literally part of your job

By play I mean “testing the models’ limits in a non-utilitarian way, with a sense of playful exploration and experimentation”

Not only is it NOT a waste of time, but I often find it to be the most productive part of my day when I look back

When working with a coding agent, instead of giving it a product spec, these days I often just describe the problem in a very open-ended way, and see what it comes up with

Usually it comes up with a better solution than I could have myself

"The journey of discovering what actually matters through iteration"

Just asked Claude to describe how the idea evolved, based on our chat history:

Here's the story:                                                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                      
The spark: your Chrome browsing history.                                                                                                                                            
You started by asking if I could access your Chrome browsing history, which is stored locally as a SQLite database. I queried it and showed you your 20 most recent entries. That  was just curiosity, not a product idea yet.                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                      
The problem articulation.                                                                                                                                                           
You described your tab management problem: "I tend to open too many tabs and never close them. Some of them are tasks that I work on and halfway through I switch to another task  and forget about the old one (might be a bit of ADHD here). The only time I actually close my tabs is when there are too many and my computer crashes." You wanted something to help you be more organized and focused.                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                      
I pitched several ideas. "Task Autopsy" (reconstructing abandoned browsing sessions), "Focus Score" (an attention fitness tracker), "Second Brain Capture" (auto-bookmarking), "The Nag" (accountability partner), "Mission Control" (live command center). You told me to keep it simple and actually useful, not dramatic.                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                      
The core concept crystallized: "One simple page that answers: what was I working on, and what did I forget about?" It reads Chrome history, uses AI to cluster browsing into tasks, and shows active vs abandoned missions.                                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                      
You asked how to make it something you'd actually use and not forget about. This was the key product insight. I suggested three options: browser homepage, morning briefing, or  Chrome extension. You picked both (A) make it the browser homepage/new tab page AND (C) a Chrome extension with a toolbar badge. This is when you discovered the new tab page is customizable -- I explained that a Chrome extension can override chrome_url_overrides.newtab to replace the default new tab with your own page.                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                      
The architecture decision: Local web app + thin Chrome extension. The extension overrides the new tab page (iframe to localhost), bridges the chrome.tabs API, and shows a badge. The server reads Chrome's SQLite history and calls DeepSeek.                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                      
I built a mockup from your real data. You saw actual missions like "Brand DNA Tool," "Voice AI Agents Research," "Phota Studio AI Portraits" clustered from your browsing. That made it real.                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                      
Then it evolved rapidly through your feedback:                                                                                                                                      
  1. You wanted tab closing (the extension can do this via chrome.tabs API)                                                                                                           
  2. You wanted every open tab accounted for, not just history matches                                                                                                                
  3. You flipped the architecture: open tabs first, history second -- the primary view should be what's currently open, not what you browsed in the past                              
  4. You killed the AI-by-default approach: static view first, AI on demand -- no tokens burned unless you click a button                                                             
  5. You added the fun factor: confetti, swoosh sound, witty AI messages                                                                                                              
  6. You kept pushing for granularity: each YouTube video is its own mission, localhost grouped by port                                                                               
  7. Community feedback killed the server: people asked "why do you need a Node.js server?" and suggested https://t.co/ggaSm4eBVR. You migrated to a pure Chrome extension with zero server  dependency.                                                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                      
The product went from "AI-powered browsing history analyzer" to "simple tab dashboard with personality." The AI got demoted from core feature to optional, then removed entirely.   
  
The server went from essential to optional to deleted. What survived: the new tab page takeover, domain grouping, satisfying close UX (swoosh + confetti), and the "save for later" checklist. The simplest version won.

tweets.text:
Find someone who genuinely cares. 

A mentor. A founder. A VC. An advisor. A customer. 

Life’s too short to work with people who don’t give a sh!t. 

It might end up being the greatest moat of them all.

Wowza so many comments.. quick things:

1) You can give system diagrams to Claude code and definitely one shot a lot of this. The point is it takes a WHILE to get to this point of clarity that AI can’t do today

2)People quoting me the bitter lesson and long horizon tasks - yes, you could be right. But AI hasn’t learned the lessons and clarity that you have. Surely, 5-10 years later with infinite compute and simulations, we could get there. 

3) Ok fine the slack notification system was not the best example. But the ethos still stand. Clarity, depth of thinking and persistence still take time.

Every time I see a tweet saying “I can vibe code this in a weekend” - I think of the slack notification system..

It takes time, persistence and effort to get the details right. 

Sure, a lot of simple workflows will get vibe coded away. And maybe you can put this in Claude Code and get the code right in one shot. 

But quality, depth and great systems will still have value and take time. You can’t vibe code lessons. 

Now and forever.

tweets.text:
That was the case in December. 4 months and thousands of work hours later, we have a great security concept; you can go all yolo, use a sandbox (Docker or OpenShell), there are allow-lists and per-access exec allow/deny prompts.

There’s hundreds of security researchers that pen-tested it.

If you look at GPT 5.4-Cyber and it's ability for closed source reverse engineering, I have bad news for you.

I do very much feel the pain though, there's hundreds of teams that try to poke holes into @openclaw. Our response has been of rapid iteration and code hardening. Which did introduce occasiaonal regression (and yes you all been yelling at me), but I see as the only way forward.

I would be very careful of other open source projects/harnesses that ignore this work and do not publish their advisories. https://t.co/NBaCouMs3i
once again, I’m amazed by scammers. https://t.co/IFJYN7EWMv

tweets.text:
The arc of a LLM skeptic:
1. They are just auto-correct
2. They are just next-token predictors
3. They hallucinate
4. They can only be useful for the data they are trained on
5. They are only good for coding
6. They are only good for verifiable things

Just admit it! LLMs are awesome.

Every day I find someone who manages to get LLMs to do something weird and interesting.

I have to keep on reminding myself that we are in the super early innings of figuring out what we can build with this magical technology.

Nikita's Cashtags announcement captured 2% of CT's mindshare in the past 24 hours. If it were a project, it would sit at #5 on the mindshare leaderboard.

So what's CT's actual reception?

The news generated 5.7M impressions across Nikita's post and the creators covering it. Opinion leaders and traders are driving most of the pickup.

Over 30 VCs and investors engaged & 7 TradFi accounts showed up. This news spans beyond crypto.

The five narratives dominating the conversation:

→ Powered by @solana (~25%): Solana's ecostystem is highly capitalizing attention on this news.

→ X as the everything app (~20%): Accounts like @jbondwagon and @AutismCapital Cashtags to X's broader fintech ambitions. Some say it makes X the default discovery layer for tokens.

→ Testing mode (~20%): A chunk of CT isn't opinionating yet. @icobeast tested $ETH, @pudgypenguins tested $PENGU, @zksync tested $ZK.

→ Skepticism (~15%): @ryandcrypto: "Cashtags are supposed to fix crypto?" @apex_ether: "Solana-only cashtags for crypto is extremely lame."

→ Feature requests (~10%): Some are already building further requests for Nikita: Hyperliquid builder codes integration, global rollout, Dogecoin support, NFT verify return.

tweets.text: jupiter market share dropped from 90% to 82% in 4 months. dflow, titan, OKX DEX eating into their routing edge. jupiter's response is building RPC 2.0 with helius, triton, and anza to vertically integrate the entire solana read layer. $150m annual revenue, $602m market cap, and they're spending to own the infrastructure underneath their own product. if RPC 2.0 ships and cuts latency 3-5x, competitors can't replicate that moat without building their own data layer. if it doesn't ship, the share bleed accelerates. JUP at $0.17 is a bet on whether a DEX aggregator can become an infrastructure company before its aggregation moat erodes completely

ethena went from 90% perps exposure to 88% T-Bills in one quarter. $15b USDe supply collapsed to $5.8b. sUSDe yields sitting at 3.5%. they survived the funding rate crisis by becoming a money market fund with extra smart contract risk. if the fed cuts to 2-3%, sUSDe yield goes sub-2% and you're taking defi complexity risk for less than a savings account. 61% of holders already left. the ones still here are short interest rates whether they know it or not

this was a great conversation (watch the damn thing) my takeaways:

- Jensen does not fuck with losers or “loser mentality”

just remembered leopold bought $250M worth of Sandisk at the end of last year…

that’s now worth $941,000,000 

it’s been 3.5 months 👍🏽 https://t.co/NjKXhxovgl https://t.co/k2Ys3W18K7

new anthropic research is a little concerning…

they discovered ai models secretly transmit behavioural traits to other models e.g. advocating for crime and violence

it’s completely invisible to human detection and fairly simple to do:

- they gave a model a trait (eg “likes owls”) then made it generate numbers

- fed those numbers into another model which then started loving owls (48% increase!)

- neither model shared the same training data. this was purely through numbers.

- researchers couldn’t figure out the number pattern. undetectable. 

- good news - it only works for models within the same family (eg claude opus and sonnet)

this is concerning because every model lab uses PREVIOUS models to train their future ones

so they could be passing on traits without humans realising it. 

def worth a read

tweets.text:
New edition of the Rekt Capital Newsletter is live

I share an Altcoin Market Update

Features analysis on the following Altcoins: $AAVE $ALGO $CRO $LTC $RENDER $SOL

Sign up to read it here:

https://t.co/HzCJOqrtxK

#BTC #Crypto #Bitcoin

#BTC

Bitcoin is now pressing beyond the resistance of the Double Bottom formation

Bitcoin would need to Weekly Close and/or post-breakout retest the top of the Double Bottom (blue $72810) to confirm a breakout

And in fact if Bitcoin fully confirms a breakout from this Double Bottom, a Measured Move could see price rally to ~$81000-$82500

But given where Bitcoin is in the market cycle however, it's likely price will develop a macro market structure that will appear sufficiently bullish only to ultimately fail over time

There are a number of ways Bitcoin could see this current overall cluster fail to generate sustained bullish momentum

The failure could occur by virtue of rejecting from the Double Bottom resistance, by failed post-breakout retest to register a fake-breakout, or by falling short of a Measured Move once the breakout is confirmed

$BTC #Crypto #Bitcoin

#BTC 

Bitcoin continues to occupy the space between two old All Time Highs from 2021 and 2024

In fact, Bitcoin has retested the 2021 ATHs as new support on the Weekly timeframe (this retest hasn't taken place on the Monthly however)

More, Bitcoin continues to reject from 2024 old ATHs

But if Bitcoin is able to Weekly Close above 2024 old ATHs, then price will have a chance to move into the high $70000s

Until that confirmation however, price will continue to be sandwiched between 2021 and 2024 old All Time Highs

$BTC #Crypto #Bitcoin
